author: @FuhrChris
text:
I understand that if you place this build on its side compared to the way it is video taped it will work with wind coming from any direction.

But What I don't see Robert attempting is to turn the displayed configuration around 180 degrees?
To the best of my ablity to understand, the blade at the bottom will NOT be aiding in rotations.
Some prodictions forecast that the blade would have negative lift and drag?
So only wind coming straight on from one side would have positive results?

Now were the build placed shaft straight up the configuration would have an optimized moment no matter how the wind hit the system or when the winds changed directions.

author: @FuhrChris
text:
When one scales this pattern up for actual usage.  One should have several material sciences considerations.
1.  The leading edges impact speeds.
The speed of the blade going North is effected by the COMBINED air speed of the dust going South.  This is marginally higher than the speed of the blades (Times 2).
1.a. This leads to the concept that the blades leading edge should be stamped stainless steel.  Keep the Whale bumps but press those shapes into Stainless steel.
2. With a protective leading edge then get an idea of the feasibility of 3D printed trailing edges that become replaceable over time.
2.a. What happens if the trailing edge gets unevenly balanced?  Sensors on the baring surface need to be in place to tell the system to shut down in case one or more of the trailing edges come off or are so worn down that they need maintenances'.

author: @RealTwiner
text:
Just had a thought about bio mimicry.  Why are featherless wings relatively uncommon, arguably an evolutionary failure. 
Feathered wings evolved multiple times and stuck around. So the question is why?

Bird wings are composed of layers of feathers,  I have to wonder if the fact that feathers dont independently completely capture all the air they encounter is a potential avenue to have more efficient air capture in a wing.

Air flows into the wing and not just over it but is ultimately all redirected in the same way. Maybe this increases the amount of captured energy from the air by the wing?

author: @hjd6170
text:
It's really interesting that the serrations seem to help with self-starting, and that's making me wonder - how big a problem is self-starting with small domestic wind turbines in variable wind conditions? If a little nudge could somehow be applied when the wind dropped in order to get it going again, would it produce a lot more energy?

A couple of ideas on self-starting:

- With a battery setup, could the generator be wired so that when the rotor blades had stopped moving but there was a bit of wind (i.e. just enough to keep blades moving but not enough to start them), it swapped into electric motor mode for a short time, to give them a push? Maybe a miniature anemometer could be used to trigger this.

- Could a mechanical device be added to store up some potential energy to keep the turbine moving when the wind dropped? Perhaps when the blades were moving above a certain speed some of the power could be directed to winding up a spring, pumping water into a raised reservoir or lifting a weight, all of which could then be released automatically to help keep the blades moving when the wind dropped.

author: @hoofheartedicemelted296
text: Some have mentioned the direction of wind flow which is a fair point but a Diffuser would redirect windflow anyway.  The Assymetric Vortex Propulsion effect is airflows version of flyback is it not Mr Smith? It starts off as a normal airflow but quickly becomes a vacuum drawing the wind in faster and faster.  The self starting definitely would have be an incentive to those folks afraid of heights which would be most people I imagine. lol. The quieter operation is appealing in and of itself, but I'm surprised the formula 1 designers have not incorporated this into their airflow designs. I read "The Shark's Paintbrush" by Jay Harman, and was fascinated by the Liliypad biomimicry using it's design to displace water droplets that literally rolled off the pad and causing the pad to maintain buoyancy and remain afloat and of course a painting company jumped all over that idea in their wall paint. Makes you wonder what other principals nature could contribute ie; the seeming weightlessness of the dragonfly due to the interaction of it's thoracic and the tiny tiny cone structures along the surface of it's wings.  Anyway just a thought sir. Still loving the videos sir.

author: @gozharry7404
text:
The principles surrounding eddy reduction via serrated trailing edges are well established, subject to scale and Re variation.

The test shown on the video is inconclusive because the fan was pounting towards the lower half of the transverse-axis projected area.

This guarantees a differential drag/lift result for opposite halves of the projected area and removes the likelihood of TSR plateau around 1.0.

A better test would point the fan at the axis of rotation, then measure and compare results for both cases, with and without serrated edges.

Good video, but imprecise engineering, unfortunately.

author: @colrodrick8784
text:
Wow!  Self starting quiet and much faster.  I call over 200rpm a huge improvement; what's that 25% better than before?.  To my mind the speed seems to be increasing the longer it was running.  That may have just been my old eyes playing tricks (which they do).  Obviously if there is an increase in speed under a constant air flow that's not going to continue.

That is already the sort of thing that could be clamped to the hull or a spar on a boat, or set up somewhere on or close to a campsite and charge up the battery for navigation or refrigeration etc.  I'd love to know what sort of voltage could be generated with what sort of wind speed, using just the little toy you have put together.

Legend Rob.  Absolute legend.

And I thought, hmm putting a Frankenstein whale/owl concept together is never going to do anything.  So, what do I know?  I know enough to not trust myself.

replies.author: @timmyssweet
replies.text:
I think Robert puts his energy into ideas, the theoretical science, rather than ridged testing.  YouTube is hard.  After all, people would patient his work if something worked, and that’s not fun for creative people. 

I think he is getting closer to a functional wind generator for the home, though.  They do look like something that one could build and save a tonne of money on with a 3D printer.  I don’t see value in a 3D printer - printing plastic.  Though, blades could easily be replaceable, and the DIY nature could get around patents. 

Perhaps, a full scale one could be printed, Robert.  You could measure the efficiency by wind speed versus electricity generated.  The, keep re-placing it with more efficient ones that you print.  After all, no commercial company will put the research into home generation - turbulent wind.

replies.author: @arhedler
replies.text: @তুহিন_জানা The issue is not the angle with the axis, because in the real world the wind can come from any direction, which is the point of vertical axis turbines. The problem with this demonstration is that the wind is only blowing on one side of the turbine, so there is no resistance coming from the other side of the axis. A proper demonstration would want a uniform mass of air hitting the whole thing, as you would find in a wind tunnel. It is possible to make a small wind tunnel using a fan at the front end which will also pull in more air, then put in baffles to reduce turbulence and provide a more uniform column of air hitting the turbine. You could also get a more uniform mass of air by moving the fan further away from the turbine so you get more air moving more uniformly across the turbine, though it would likely be at lower velocity.
